description: Figure 1 presents comparative bar charts showing room-temperature mechanical properties of Fe-Al-M-C alloys (M = Ti, V, Nb, Ta). Panel (a) displays compressive yield stress (dark gray bars, MPa) and matrix Brinell hardness (light gray bars, HB) for Fe-15Al-based alloys, including binary Fe-15Al and variants with 2Ti-1C, 2V-1C, 2Nb-1C, and 2Ta-1C additions. Panel (b) presents analogous data for Fe-26Al-based alloys, covering binary Fe-26Al and compositions with 2Ti-1C, 4Ti-1C, 2V-1C, 2Nb-1C, 4Nb-1C, and 2Ta-1C. The data reveals that Nb and Ta additions provide the most significant strengthening in Fe-15Al alloys, achieving yield stresses of approximately 827 MPa and 868 MPa respectively, compared to 356 MPa for the binary alloy. For Fe-26Al alloys, the 4Nb-1C composition exhibits the highest yield strength (~792 MPa). However, citation sentences note that while Fe-15Al-based alloys with Ti and V show good correlation between hardness and yield stress, most Fe-26Al-based alloys exhibit no such correlation, with matrix hardness values failing to predict compressive yield strength trends.

Figure 2 presents engineering stress–strain curves obtained from room-temperature compression testing of Fe–Al–M–C alloys, comparing two base compositions: Fe–15Al-based alloys in panel (a) and Fe–26Al-based alloys in panel (b). Subfigure (a) displays curves for five alloys: binary Fe–15Al (curve 1), Fe–15Al–2Ti–1C (2), Fe–15Al–2V–1C (3), Fe–15Al–2Nb–1C (4), and Fe–15Al–2Ta–1C (5), with solid circles marking the 0.2% offset yield strength (σ₀.₂). Subfigure (b) shows seven Fe–26Al-based alloys: Fe–26Al (6), Fe–26Al–2Ti–1C (7), Fe–26Al–4Ti–1C (8), Fe–26Al–2V–1C (9), Fe–26Al–2Nb–1C (10), Fe–26Al–4Nb–1C (11), and Fe–26Al–2Ta–1C (12). The curves reveal that additions of Nb or Ta provide the highest strengthening effects, with Fe–15Al–2Ta–1C and Fe–15Al–2Nb–1C exhibiting σ₀.₂ values around 868 MPa and 827 MPa respectively, significantly exceeding the binary Fe–15Al alloy (~356 MPa). The Fe–26Al-based alloys show pronounced work hardening with nearly constant hardening rates before reaching a parabolic stage, and despite lower initial yield stresses than the binary alloy for most compositions, they exhibit much stronger hardening during plastic deformation.

Figure 3 presents room-temperature tensile engineering stress–strain curves for Fe–Al–M–C alloys (M = Ti, V, Nb, Ta), comparing two base compositions: Fe–15Al (subfigure a) and Fe–26Al (subfigure b). Subfigure (a) displays five curves: (1) binary Fe-15Al showing the lowest strength (~350 MPa) with significant ductility; (2) Fe-15Al-2Ti-1C with moderate strengthening; (3) Fe-15Al-2V-1C exhibiting limited ductility; (4) Fe-15Al-2Nb-1C and (5) Fe-15Al-2Ta-1C demonstrating the highest strengthening effects with yield stresses exceeding 700 MPa and showing sharp yielding behavior. Subfigure (b) shows seven curves for the Fe-26Al system: (6) Fe-26Al; (7) Fe-26Al-2Ti-1C; (8) Fe-26Al-4Ti-1C; (9) Fe-26Al-2V-1C; (10) Fe-26Al-2Nb-1C; (11) Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C; and (12) Fe-26Al-2Ta-1C. The Fe-26Al-2Ti-1C alloy (curve 7) exhibits the highest strength in this series, while most Fe-26Al-based alloys show limited ductility compared to their Fe-15Al counterparts. Solid circles mark the 0.2% offset yield strength (σ₀.₂) for key alloys, and a scale bar indicates 2.5% strain. These curves demonstrate that Nb and Ta carbide-forming additions provide superior room-temperature strengthening compared to Ti and V in both alloy systems, though typically at the expense of ductility.

This figure presents a comprehensive set of scanning electron microscopy (SEM) fractographs showing the fracture surfaces of Fe–Al–M–C (M = Ti, V, Nb, Ta) alloys after tensile testing at room temperature, with all images captured at the same magnification (scale bar: 10 μm). The binary Fe–15Al alloy (Fig. 4a) exhibits transgranular fracture with very fine cleavage facets corresponding to its 16% ductility. The Fe–15Al–2M–1C series (Fig. 4b–e) displays coarse transgranular cleavage reflecting brittle behavior, with Fe–15Al–2Ti–1C showing the highest ductility (~3.3%) among this group. The binary Fe–26Al alloy (Fig. 4f) shows very coarse transgranular fracture features. In the Fe–26Al–2M–1C series, Fe–26Al–2Nb–1C (Fig. 4j) presents fine facetted transgranular cleavage correlating with its superior ductility (~1.4%), while Fe–26Al–2V–1C (Fig. 4i) reveals uniform steps indicating transgranular fracture influenced by the crystallographic orientation of plate-like vanadium carbide precipitates. The Fe–26Al-based alloys with Ti or Ta additions (Fig. 4g, h, l) exhibit mixed fracture modes combining intergranular decohesion (smooth areas) and transgranular cleavage (facetted areas), where cube-like TiC precipitates are clearly distinguishable on intergranular fracture surfaces. Notably, Fe–26Al–4Nb–1C (Fig. 4k) shows intergranular decohesion alongside transgranular cleavage, with densely distributed precipitates roughening the decohesion areas. These micrographs collectively demonstrate the transition from ductile fine-scale cleavage to brittle coarse cleavage and intergranular failure as carbide-forming elements are added, correlating directly with the observed room-temperature mechanical properties and brittleness.

Figure 5 presents the temperature dependence of compression yield stress (0.2% offset) for Fe-15Al-based alloys measured from room temperature up to 800°C. The graph plots yield stress in MPa against temperature in °C for five material systems: binary Fe-15Al (baseline), Fe-15Al-2Ti-1C, Fe-15Al-2V-1C, Fe-15Al-2Nb-1C, and Fe-15Al-2Ta-1C. All curves exhibit a gradual monotonous decrease in yield strength with increasing temperature. The alloys containing Nb or Ta demonstrate the highest strength retention across the entire temperature range, starting from approximately 827 MPa and 868 MPa at ambient temperature and declining to about 83 MPa and 104 MPa at 800°C, respectively. In contrast, the binary Fe-15Al alloy shows the lowest strength values, dropping from roughly 356 MPa to 32 MPa over the same interval. The caption states these tests were conducted at elevated temperatures up to 800°C with a 30-minute hold at testing temperature before loading. This data supports the conclusion that MC carbide precipitation strengthening is particularly effective for Nb- and Ta-containing compositions, indicating their potential as high-temperature structural materials.

This figure comprises two subplots showing the temperature dependence of compression yield stress for Fe-26Al-based alloys from room temperature to 800°C. Subplot (a) presents data for binary Fe-26Al (open squares) compared with Ti- and V-containing alloys: Fe-26Al-2Ti-1C (diamonds), Fe-26Al-4Ti-1C (circles), and Fe-26Al-2V-1C (triangles). Subplot (b) shows the same baseline Fe-26Al alongside Nb- and Ta-containing alloys: Fe-26Al-2Nb-1C (triangles), Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C (asterisks), and Fe-26Al-2Ta-1C (crosses). All Fe-26Al-based alloys exhibit an anomalous yield strength increase at intermediate temperatures, peaking around 550°C at approximately 400 MPa for Ti- and V-containing alloys in (a). In (b), the yield stress anomaly peaks for Nb- and Ta-containing alloys shift toward lower temperatures and higher stresses compared to the binary alloy, with Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C showing the most significant shift to 474°C with a peak stress of 619 MPa. The curves demonstrate that precipitation strengthening via MC carbides and Laves phase (Fe,Al)₂Nb effectively enhances high-temperature strength, with the Laves phase providing superior strengthening at elevated temperatures due to higher thermal stability.

Bar charts comparing yield stresses measured at 650 °C in compression (dark gray bars) and tension (light gray bars) for two series of iron aluminide alloys: Fe–15Al-based alloys (subfigure a) and Fe–26Al-based alloys (subfigure b). Subfigure (a) presents data for binary Fe-15Al and quaternary alloys with 2 at.% Ti, V, Nb, or Ta additions plus 1 at.% C (Fe-15Al-2Ti-1C, Fe-15Al-2V-1C, Fe-15Al-2Nb-1C, Fe-15Al-2Ta-1C), showing yield stresses ranging from approximately 80–120 MPa for the binary alloy to 330–430 MPa for Nb/Ta-containing alloys in tension, with consistently higher values in compression. Subfigure (b) displays Fe-26Al binary alloy and alloys with varying Ti (2 or 4 at.%), V (2 at.%), Nb (2 or 4 at.%), and Ta (2 at.%), with carbon additions, showing yield stresses from approximately 95–155 MPa for the binary alloy up to 210–330 MPa for higher Nb-content alloys. The data visually demonstrates pronounced tension–compression asymmetry across all compositions, with compression yield stresses typically exceeding tensile values by 30–100 MPa. The charts reveal that Nb and Ta additions provide the most significant strengthening effects at 650 °C compared to Ti or V additions, and that Fe-26Al-based alloys generally exhibit higher absolute yield strengths than their Fe-15Al counterparts.

This figure presents bar charts comparing the yield stresses measured at 800°C in both compression (dark bars) and tension (light bars) for Fe-Al-M-C alloys strengthened by carbides. Subfigure (a) displays data for Fe-15Al-based alloys, including the base alloy Fe-15Al and variants alloyed with 2% Ti, V, Nb, or Ta plus 1% carbon. Subfigure (b) shows corresponding data for Fe-26Al-based alloys, encompassing the base Fe-26Al and modified compositions with varying additions of Ti (2% and 4%), V, Nb (2% and 4%), or Ta. The horizontal axis indicates yield stress in MPa from 0 to 140. The data reveal a pronounced tension-compression asymmetry across all compositions, with compressive yield strengths consistently exceeding tensile values by varying margins. Carbide-forming additions significantly enhance high-temperature strength compared to the base alloys, with Nb and Ta providing the most effective strengthening. The Fe-26Al-based alloys generally exhibit higher yield stresses than their Fe-15Al counterparts, with Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C showing the highest values (~136 MPa in compression, ~82 MPa in tension). These results confirm the asymmetric deformation behavior and demonstrate that precipitation strengthening via MC carbides effectively improves elevated-temperature mechanical performance.
